title: Credentials, Signing, Retries, Timeouts, and Client Resilience · Language Lens: Idempotency Reconciliation
summary: A process accepts static keys from request data, retries every failure beyond its caller deadline, and records neither attempt identity nor ambiguous write state. Predict the representation, storage, trust, cost, and recovery evidence before inspecting a model. Planned artifact: Add and verify the idempotency reconciliation increment in bounded S3 request and retry state machine. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Make stable identity, conditional requests, upload records, checksums, and post-failure reconciliation explicit for repeatable operations.
Classify throttling, transient transport, server, permanent, cancellation, conflict, and ambiguous write outcomes with bounded attempts, time, delay, and bytes.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and Go Predict Idempotency Reconciliation · Theory Idempotency Reconciliation
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory Go file-delivery team handles case 3398 during a cost-anomaly investigation. Build deterministic Go evidence to classify S3 outcomes and bound retries while preserving write ambiguity; retain stable tenant and file identity without exposing it in metric labels; then double concurrent transfers and keep process resource limits intact. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, databases, credentials, host state, race and leak checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Make stable identity, conditional requests, upload records, checksums, and post-failure reconciliation explicit for repeatable operations.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Idempotency Reconciliation contract for the supplied data.
